private List<String> combinations;
private int currentIndex;

In the constructor, generate the combinations. Then next() returns combinations[currentIndex++] and hasNext checks if currentIndex < combinations.size().

For example, a backtracking method:

private void generateCombinations(List<String> combinations, StringBuilder current, int start, int combinationLength, String characters) {
    if (current.length() == combinationLength) {
        combinations.add(current.toString());
        return;
    }
    for (int i = start; i < characters.length(); i++) {
        current.append(characters.charAt(i));
        generateCombinations(combinations, current, i + 1, combinationLength, characters);
        current.deleteCharAt(current.length() - 1);
    }
}

This way, the combinations are generated in lex order. Because for each step, we choose the next character in the characters string, ensuring that each combination is built in order. Since the characters are already sorted, this approach will generate the combinations in lex order.

So in the constructor, call this method to fill the combinations list.

public CombinationIterator(String characters, int combinationLength) {
    this.combinations = new ArrayList<>();
    generateCombinations(combinations, new StringBuilder(), 0, combinationLength, characters);
    this.currentIndex = 0;
}

class CombinationIterator {
    private List<String> combinations;
    private int currentIndex;
    
    public CombinationIterator(String characters, int combinationLength) {
        combinations = new ArrayList<>();
        generate(combinations, new StringBuilder(), 0, combinationLength, characters);
        currentIndex = 0;
    }
    
    private void generate(List<String> list, StringBuilder current, int start, int combinationLength, String characters) {
        if (current.length() == combinationLength) {
            list.add(current.toString());
            return;
        }
        for (int i = start; i < characters.length(); i++) {
            current.append(characters.charAt(i));
            generate(list, current, i + 1, combinationLength, characters);
            current.deleteCharAt(current.length() - 1);
        }
    }
    
    public String next() {
        return combinations.get(currentIndex++);
    }
    
    public boolean hasNext() {
        return currentIndex < combinations.size();
    }
}

solution: 
```java
class CombinationIterator {
    private List<String> combinations;
    private int currentIndex;

    public CombinationIterator(String characters, int combinationLength) {
        combinations = new ArrayList<>();
        generate(combinations, new StringBuilder(), 0, combinationLength, characters);
        currentIndex = 0;
    }

    private void generate(List<String> list, StringBuilder current, int start, int combinationLength, String characters) {
        if (current.length() == combinationLength) {
            list.add(current.toString());
            return;
        }
        for (int i = start; i < characters.length(); i++) {
            current.append(characters.charAt(i));
            generate(list, current, i + 1, combinationLength, characters);
            current.deleteCharAt(current.length() - 1);
        }
    }

    public String next() {
        return combinations.get(currentIndex++);
    }
    
    public boolean hasNext() {
        return currentIndex < combinations.size();
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1) for next and hasNext, O(C(n, k) * k) for initialization
space complexity: O(C(n, k) * k)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Precomputes all valid combinations in lexicographical order during initialization using backtracking. next() and hasNext() operate in O(1) by tracking an index. Strengths: fast subsequent calls. Weakness: higher memory usage for large input.
